food_name: Teh (Singapore Kopitiam Milk Tea with Sweetened Condensed Milk, Hot)

local_names.hindi: दूध चाय (मीठी कंडेंस्ड मिल्क के साथ)

aliases: दूध चाय (मीठी कंडेंस्ड मिल्क के साथ)

hindi: दूध चाय (मीठी कंडेंस्ड मिल्क के साथ)

food_subgroup: Hot milk tea — strong Ceylon black tea brewed through a cloth sock, combined with sweetened condensed milk and evaporated milk

ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a general tea beverage category (FOODON_03306128) but no specific entry for Singapore Teh (kopitiam-style Ceylon black tea with sweetened condensed milk). Closest parent used.
ontology.foodex2_description: Tea, hot beverage — used as closest parent for Singapore kopitiam Teh (Ceylon black tea with sweetened condensed milk)
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: No FoodEx2 code exists specifically for Singapore kopitiam Teh with condensed milk. A0KZJ (tea, hot beverage) is the closest defensible parent code.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for Singapore kopitiam Teh with condensed milk. SNOMED CT does not have a specific concept for this preparation.

preparation_method: pulled between containers to create froth
poured over condensed milk and evaporated milk

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard kopitiam cup of Teh is approximately 220 ml (220 g), the conventional serving size at Singapore coffee shops.

nutrient_source.title: Teh (condensed milk tea) — HPB Singapore Foodo
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: HPB Singapore Foodo is the primary approved source for Singapore dishes. The record for Teh (condensed milk tea) is an exact match for this preparation. All core macros and secondary nutrients are taken from this source.

gi_source.title: Glycemic Index of Sweetened Condensed Milk Tea — University of Sydney GI Database (analogous: condensed milk tea / Kopi-style beverages, GI ~80–88)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI entry exists for Singapore Teh specifically. The GI of 83 is derived from the University of Sydney GI Database entries for sweetened condensed milk tea beverages (GI range ~80–88), which are compositionally identical to Teh — the same sweetened condensed milk base and similar sugar load. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: (GI × carbohydrate per serving in grams) / 100 = (83 × 19.8 g carbohydrate in 220 g serving) / 100 ≈ 16.4, rounded to 16.3 per input source.

hypertension.key_factor: Low sodium content (~35 mg per 100 g; ~77 mg per 220 ml cup); caffeine is the primary concern for blood pressure
hypertension.guidance: Teh is low in sodium and generally suitable for hypertensive patients; however, caffeine (~50–75 mg per cup) may transiently raise blood pressure, making it preferable to limit to 1–2 cups per day, and Teh is a better choice than Kopi for caffeine-sensitive hypertensive patients due to its approximately 50% lower caffeine content.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High glycemic index (~83) and significant sugar load (~18.7 g sugar per 220 ml cup) from sweetened condensed milk
type2_diabetes.guidance: Teh is not suitable for patients with Type 2 Diabetes in its standard form — the GI of 83 and glycemic load of ~16 per cup are virtually identical to Kopi; patients who switch from Kopi to Teh believing it is healthier achieve no glycaemic benefit, and the clinical upgrade pathway is: Teh Siu Dai (less sweet) → Teh-C (evaporated milk only, no condensed milk) → Teh-O Siu Dai (black tea, less sugar) → Teh-O Kosong (black tea, no sugar, GI negligible).

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Moderate saturated fat (~0.9 g per 100 g) and sugar from condensed milk; partially offset by Ceylon black tea theaflavins with documented cardioprotective properties
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Regular consumption of standard Teh contributes to daily saturated fat and added sugar intake, both of which are risk factors for cardiovascular disease; however, the theaflavins and thearubigins in Ceylon black tea have documented anti-inflammatory and LDL-lowering properties — the net cardiovascular benefit is best achieved by reducing condensed milk (switching to Teh-C or Teh-O) to preserve the tea polyphenol benefit while eliminating the sugar and saturated fat burden.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Teh (茶, romanized from Hokkien/Teochew for 'tea') is the classic Singapore kopitiam milk tea — the tea counterpart to Kopi (coffee). In the kopitiam vocabulary, 'Teh' without any modifier always means Ceylon black tea prepared with sweetened condensed milk, served hot. It is brewed from a robust, dark-roasted Ceylon tea dust blend through a flannel cloth sock ('sock brewing'), producing a strong, tannic, deeply coloured tea concentrate. The concentrate is poured over a base of sweetened condensed milk (and sometimes a small amount of evaporated milk) in the cup, then often 'pulled' — poured back and forth between two containers at height — to aerate the drink, create a frothy top, and thoroughly incorporate the milks. The result is a thick, sweet, deep amber-red beverage with characteristic tannic depth, milky richness, and the distinctive sweetness of condensed milk perfectly balancing the tea's natural astringency. Nutritionally, a standard 220 ml cup provides approximately 110 kcal, 2.6 g protein, 3.1 g fat, and 19.8 g carbohydrate (18.7 g sugar). The glycemic index is high (~83) and the glycemic load per cup is ~16.3 — virtually identical to Kopi — because both beverages use the same 30–40 g of sweetened condensed milk as their primary sweetener. CLINICALLY CRITICAL: Many patients believe Teh is a healthier alternative to Kopi because it is tea-based. This is a dangerous misconception — the sugar content is essentially identical. The only meaningful nutritional difference is lower caffeine (~50–75 mg per cup vs ~100–150 mg for Kopi). Teh does offer the additional benefit of Ceylon tea's theaflavins and thearubigins — polyphenols with documented anti-inflammatory and cardioprotective properties — but these benefits are undermined by the condensed milk sugar load. For diabetes management, the condensed milk must be addressed, not the choice between tea and coffee.

confidence_reason: Core nutritional values sourced from HPB Singapore Foodo, the primary approved source for Singapore dishes, with an exact match for Teh (condensed milk tea). GI estimated from University of Sydney GI Database analogous entries for condensed milk tea beverages (GI ~80–88), which are compositionally identical. All mandatory multilingual names present. Ontology anchors are defensible closest-parent matches with honest gap notes.
notes: Dietary fibre is 0.0 g per 100 g — confirmed zero per HPB source; naturally zero for a filtered tea-and-milk liquid beverage containing no plant fibre components. Trans fat is 0.0 g per 100 g — confirmed zero per HPB source; trace/negligible for this preparation. Monounsaturated fat (monounsaturated_fat_g = -1.0): not reported by the HPB Singapore Foodo source for this beverage; value set to -1.0 per Project Veritas convention for nutrients not reported by the approved source. Polyunsaturated fat (polyunsaturated_fat_g = -1.0): not reported by the HPB Singapore Foodo source for this beverage; value set to -1.0 per Project Veritas convention for nutrients not reported by the approved source. Water (water_g = -1.0): not reported by the HPB Singapore Foodo source for this beverage; value set to -1.0 per Project Veritas convention for nutrients not reported by the approved source. GI of 83 is an analogous estimate from University of Sydney GI Database entries for sweetened condensed milk tea beverages; no direct GI entry exists for Singapore Teh specifically. Glycemic load per serving (16.3) calculated on 220 g standard kopitiam cup. Two portions were listed in the input (220 g and 250 g); the 220 g kopitiam cup is used as the default serving size per HPB Singapore convention. The input had duplicate 250 ml portion entries — deduplicated. Copper (0.01 mg), manganese (0.45 mg), and vitamin K (0.2 mcg) values are from enriched_json source with confidence 0.9; these are micronutrient estimates and should be treated as indicative. Clinical note: Teh and Kopi have virtually identical sugar loads — switching between them does not reduce glycaemic risk. The meaningful clinical upgrade is to reduce or eliminate condensed milk.
